title: SELECT Statement
content:
### SELECT Statement

The SELECT statement is the most commonly used SQL command. It retrieves data from one or more tables.

---

### Basic SELECT Syntax

```sql
-- Select all columns
SELECT * FROM employees;

-- Select specific columns
SELECT name, salary FROM employees;

-- Select with expressions
SELECT name, salary * 1.1 AS increased_salary FROM employees;
```

### SELECT * vs Specific Columns

Using `SELECT *` returns all columns from the table. It is convenient for quick exploration but should be avoided in production code because it may return unnecessary data and break when the table schema changes.

| Approach | When to Use |
|----------|------------|
| SELECT * | Quick exploration, ad-hoc queries |
| Specific columns | Production code, application queries |

### Column Aliases

Use the AS keyword to give a column a temporary name:

```sql
SELECT name AS employee_name, salary AS annual_salary
FROM employees;
```

> **Key Point:** SELECT does not modify the data in the database. It only retrieves it.

quiz.question: What does SELECT * FROM employees do?
options: Returns all columns and all rows from the employees table

quiz.question: Which keyword is used to give a column a temporary name in the result set?
